title: Einstein's Cosmological Constant Decision and Retraction
description: Documentation of Einstein's introduction of the cosmological constant, his reasoning process, and later decision to call it his 'greatest mistake.' Shows error correction and intellectual humility.

title: Einstein's Patent Office Years: Decision-Making Under Constraint
description: Analysis of how Einstein made scientific breakthroughs while working as a patent clerk. Reveals his ability to prioritize, manage time, and think independently under institutional constraints.

title: Einstein's Struggle with Quantum Theory: The Einstein-Podolsky-Rosen Paper Analysis
description: Documents Einstein's reasoning through his fundamental disagreement with quantum mechanics, showing how he weighed scientific values (determinism vs. empirical evidence) and made decisions about theory acceptance.

title: Einstein on Politics and Civil Rights: Letters and Speeches
description: Einstein's reasoning through political decisions, including pacifism vs. supporting WWII, civil rights advocacy, and McCarthyism. Shows how he applied scientific thinking to social problems.

title: Einstein's Notebooks on Unified Field Theory (1920s-1950s)
description: Working notebooks showing Einstein's failed attempts at unified field theory. Reveals his persistence patterns, how he handled repeated failures, and his decision-making about when to continue vs. abandon approaches.

title: Einstein's Letters to Michele Besso (1903-1955)
description: Decades of correspondence with his closest friend and scientific confidant. Contains Einstein's unguarded thoughts on scientific problems, decision-making processes, and philosophical struggles. Shows his reasoning through major theories including relativity.

title: The Born-Einstein Letters: Correspondence between Albert Einstein and Max and Hedwig Born from 1916-1955
description: Intimate correspondence revealing Einstein's thought processes about quantum mechanics, his famous 'God does not play dice' reasoning, and his decision-making framework for evaluating scientific theories.

title: Einstein's Letters to Solovine (1906-1955)
description: Correspondence with Maurice Solovine spanning 50 years. Shows Einstein's philosophical reasoning, how he thought through scientific and ethical problems, and his evolving worldview.

title: The Travel Diaries of Albert Einstein (1922-1923)
description: Personal diary entries during travels to Asia. Contains unfiltered observations and reasoning about culture, science, and personal values. Shows spontaneous decision-making and judgment patterns.

title: Einstein's Religious and Philosophical Views: Letters to Various Correspondents
description: Collection of letters discussing Einstein's reasoning about God, religion, and philosophy. Shows how he integrated scientific and philosophical thinking, particularly his 'cosmic religious feeling' framework.

title: Einstein's Kyoto Address (1922) - How I Created the Theory of Relativity
description: Einstein's own detailed account of his reasoning process and decision-making steps that led to special and general relativity. Rare first-person narrative of scientific thinking.

title: Einstein's Princeton Lectures: The Meaning of Relativity (1921)
description: Einstein's own explanation of his reasoning behind relativity theory. Shows his pedagogical thinking and how he structured complex reasoning for others to follow.

title: Conversations with Einstein by Alexander Moszkowski (1920)
description: Direct conversations with Einstein during his early fame period. Captures his spontaneous reasoning about science, philosophy, and life decisions. Shows unscripted thinking patterns.

title: Subtle is the Lord: The Science and Life of Albert Einstein by Abraham Pais
description: Written by a physicist who knew Einstein personally. Combines scientific analysis with personal observations of Einstein's thinking patterns and decision-making approaches.

title: Einstein: His Life and Universe by Walter Isaacson
description: Comprehensive analysis of Einstein's decision-making patterns using extensive primary sources. Particularly strong on his reasoning through scientific problems and personal decisions.
